summary: A constant-outcome sum-comparison oracle must distinguish one exceptional index among n candidates, giving a logarithmic information lower bound, while equal-half binary search matches it and the 20-query contract excludes a genuine slower legal query class.

optimality.argument: Each compareSub call has only three possible outcomes and therefore supplies constant information; distinguishing n possible exceptional positions requires Omega(log n) calls. The accepted algorithm halves the candidate interval after every nonterminal call and uses at most ceil(log2(n)) calls.

replacement_checks.evidence: A decision tree with constant branching factor needs logarithmic depth to distinguish n possible large-value indices.
Equal-size half comparisons cancel the common values and reduce the candidate interval by at least half, using at most 19 calls for n <= 500000.
Regression checks every exceptional position through length 128, randomized larger arrays, odd leftovers, both endpoints, and the 500000-element maximum under the 20-call cap.
